 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.3","title":"[FCP2-CONF] Interop Test Suite (Handshake, Symbols, Tokens, Cross-Zone)","description":"# [FCP2-CONF] Interop Test Suite (Handshake, Symbols, Tokens, Cross-Zone)

## Goal
Verify that independent implementations can interoperate on the wire.

Even though the reference implementation is Rust, these tests should be structured so a second implementation (future Rust rewrite, Go/Python test client, etc.) can plug in.

## Scenarios (minimum)
### Sessions / handshake
- MeshSessionHello/Ack transcript verification:
  - deterministic transcript bytes
  - suite negotiation determinism
- HelloRetry cookie path:
  - responder issues cookie
  - initiator retries with cookie
- TransportLimits negotiation + enforcement:
  - max_datagram_bytes affects FCPS_DATAGRAM acceptance

### FCPS data plane
- FCPS_DATAGRAM envelope:
  - MAC computation and verification for Suite1 and Suite2
  - bounded replay window behavior
  - MTU enforcement
- Inner FCPS frame parsing:
  - header fields parsed exactly
  - symbol record parsing with strict bounds
- Per-symbol AEAD:
  - deterministic nonce derivation
  - AAD binding matches spec

### FCPC control plane
- FCPC frame parsing/ordering:
  - ordered delivery per direction
  - bounded replay window
  - k_ctx AEAD integrity (and confidentiality if enabled)

### Capability tokens + provable authority
- COSE_Sign1 encoding/verification
- grant_object_ids subset enforcement
- chk_id/chk_seq freshness binding
- holder_node + holder_proof verification

### Cross-zone enforcement
- deny cross-zone operations without required ApprovalToken
- allow with correct ApprovalToken evidence
- DecisionReceipt reason_code + evidence stability

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests are deterministic and can run in CI without a real tailnet.
- The test harness can ingest golden vectors as inputs and validate byte-for-byte expectations.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.4","title":"[FCP2-CONF] System E2E Harness: Multi-Node Simulation + Structured Logs","description":"# [FCP2-CONF] System E2E Harness: Multi-Node Simulation + Structured Logs

## Goal
Provide end-to-end confidence that the platform works as a coherent system.

## Key idea
Run a multi-node simulation (in-process or multi-process) with deterministic identities and keys, so we can test:
- sessions
- symbol routing
- policy decisions
- receipts/audit
- repair loop behavior

## Deliverables
- A harness that can spin up N MeshNodes with deterministic keys.
- A "minimal connector" (or test connector) that exercises invoke + events.
- Scripts/tests that run the required E2E scenarios from STANDARD.

## Logging
- Every scenario emits structured JSON logs with correlation_id and phase.

## Acceptance Criteria
- CI can run the E2E harness with `--nocapture` and produce usable logs.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.5","title":"[FCP2-CONF] Mechanical Connector Compliance Runner (Static + Dynamic)","description":"# [FCP2-CONF] Mechanical Connector Compliance Runner (Static + Dynamic)

## Goal
Given a connector binary, mechanically verify it meets the FCP2 connector contract.

## Static checks
- `--manifest` output parses and validates strictly.
- Manifest versioning fields are present and sane:
  - `schema_version`, `min_mesh_version`, `min_protocol`, `interface_hash`
- Canonical IDs and schema versions.
- Capability declarations are explicit (required/optional/forbidden).
- Zone constraints (`home`, allowed_sources/targets, forbidden) are present.
- Sandbox profile + network constraints are within zone ceilings.

## Dynamic checks
Run the connector under the host/SDK harness and probe:
- standard methods (handshake/describe/introspect/capabilities/configure/invoke/health/shutdown)
- **optional** preflight: if connector exposes `simulate`, probe simulate for at least one operation and verify:
  - response is well-formed and deterministic
  - no side effects are observed in the harness (no receipts/intents, no state mutation beyond caches)
- capability gating (default deny)
- DecisionReceipt production on denials (stable reason_code + evidence)
- event subscription/replay behavior (if declared)
- no secret leakage in logs

## Acceptance Criteria
- Compliance runner can be used in CI to accept/reject a connector artifact.


LABELS: conformance, connectors, fcp2, mvp

-{"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.6","title":"[FCP2-CONF] FZPF v0.1 JSON Schema Pack (Policy Authoring + Validation)","description":"# [FCP2-CONF] FZPF v0.1 JSON Schema Pack (Policy Authoring + Validation)

## Goal
Make policy authoring **mechanical and verifiable** by shipping the **FZPF v0.1 JSON Schema** (Spec Appendix A) as an explicit artifact, with tests.

Even though FCP2’s canonical objects are deterministic CBOR, users and tools still need a **human-authorable** representation for zone/policy definitions.

## Scope
- Define/ship `FZPF_v0.1.schema.json` (or equivalent) in-repo.
- Provide golden example documents that cover the important policy shapes.
- Validate that policy JSON inputs can be validated deterministically and fail-closed.

## Design Notes
- This is **V2-only**. Any older “hub” concepts are out of scope.
- FZPF should be aligned to the V2 policy object model:
  - ZoneDefinitionObject
  - ZonePolicyObject
  - RoleObject / RoleAssignment
  - ApprovalToken constraints (JSON Pointer only)
  - ZoneTransportPolicy and port-gating fields

## Deliverables
- JSON schema file for FZPF (v0.1).
- Example policy documents:
  - minimal zone definition + policy
  - role bundles + assignment + attenuation
  - transport restrictions (DERP/Funnel forbidden)
  - revocation/checkpoint freshness policy (Strict/Warn/BestEffort)
  - taint/approval constraints (elevation + declassification)
- Unit tests:
  - validate example docs against schema
  - negative tests for forbidden constructs (regex/JSONPath, unbounded globs, unknown fields)
  - deterministic ordering and stable validation errors (no non-deterministic maps)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Schema is present in the repo and referenced by conformance docs.
- Example docs validate and are comprehensive enough to exercise the sharp edges.
- Validation is fail-closed: unknown/extra fields are rejected.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.22","title":"[FCP2] CLI: install/doctor/explain/bench/audit/repair","description":"# [FCP2] CLI: install/doctor/explain/bench/audit/repair

## Goal
Ship an operator/dev CLI that makes the system operable **without disabling security**.

Key principle: if you can't explain a denial or quantify offline availability, the system isn't finished.

## Command set (MVP)
### Safety + debugging
- `fcp doctor --zone <zone>`
  - report checkpoint freshness, revocation freshness, degraded-mode state
  - report transport policy state (DERP/Funnel allowed?)
- `fcp explain --request <objectid>`
  - render DecisionReceipt evidence and reason_code
  - include pointers to relevant objects (token, grants, checkpoint, approvals)

### Storage + availability
- `fcp repair status --zone <zone>`
  - report coverage metrics (bps), distinct nodes, max concentration
  - report pending/last repair actions
- `fcp audit tail --zone <zone>`
  - tail AuditEvent stream with trace correlation

### Install + verification
- `fcp install <connector>`
  - verify supply chain chain and policy requirements
  - install/mirror connector into mesh store

### Performance
- `fcp bench ...`
  - canonical CBOR encode/decode
  - ObjectId derivation
  - token verify
  - FCPS parse+verify
  - RaptorQ decode
  - invoke-local and invoke-mesh

## Output contracts
- All commands MUST support a machine-readable `--json` output mode.
- Errors MUST be stable FCP-XXXX codes with retry semantics.
- Commands MUST NOT print secrets (including auth headers, tokens, and raw secret material).

## Testing
- Snapshot tests for `--json` output schemas.
- E2E tests in the deterministic harness:
  - `fcp install` + invoke
  - `fcp explain` on a denial
  - `fcp doctor` showing stale/fresh transitions
  - `fcp repair status` showing coverage changes

## Acceptance Criteria
- CLI commands operate purely from mesh objects and verifiable receipts.
- A denial can be diagnosed from DecisionReceipt + CLI output alone.

## Implementation note
The concrete CLI implementation work is tracked in the child bead `flywheel_connectors-iqrb`.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.23","title":"[FCP2] Performance Budgets: Benchmarks, Profiles, Binary Size Targets","description":"# [FCP2] Performance Budgets: Benchmarks, Profiles, Binary Size Targets

## Goal
Make performance and binary size regressions **visible, measurable, and hard to ignore**.

The benchmark suite is part of the product: if we can’t measure cold start, invoke latency, decode costs, and offline-repair overhead, we can’t keep the system fast and reliable.

## Canonical Targets (README-aligned)
Track these as p50/p99 where applicable:
- **Cold start (connector activate):** p50 < 100ms / p99 < 500ms (stretch goal: p50 < 50ms)
- **Local invoke latency (same node):** p50 < 2ms / p99 < 10ms
- **Tailnet invoke latency (LAN/direct):** p50 < 20ms / p99 < 100ms
- **Tailnet invoke latency (DERP):** p50 < 150ms / p99 < 500ms
- **Symbol reconstruction (1MB):** p50 < 50ms / p99 < 250ms
- **Secret reconstruction (k-of-n):** p50 < 150ms / p99 < 750ms
- **Memory overhead:** < 10MB per connector (idle)
- **CPU overhead:** < 1% idle (event-driven)
- **Binary size:** < 20MB compressed (release profile: opt-level=z, lto, strip)

## Bench Suite Shape (CLI contract)
The reference implementation ships `fcp bench` with stable subcommands:
- `fcp bench connector-activate`
- `fcp bench invoke-local`
- `fcp bench invoke-mesh --path=direct`
- `fcp bench invoke-mesh --path=derp`
- `fcp bench raptorq --size=1mb`
- `fcp bench secrets --k=3 --n=5`

Additionally, we want microbenches for hot primitives:
- canonical CBOR encode/decode
- ObjectId derivation
- CapabilityToken verify
- FCPS parse + session MAC verify

## Output + Regression Tracking
- Emit machine-readable JSON results (with environment metadata + versions + commit hash).
- Provide deterministic bench fixtures where possible.
- CI policy can start as “report-only” and later move to strict thresholds once baselines stabilize.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Bench suite runs in CI (or as a gated job) and outputs machine-readable JSON.
- The CLI subcommand surface is stable enough to track regressions over time.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.24","title":"[FCP2] Sessions: MeshSessionHello/Ack, Suite Negotiation, Key Schedule, Anti-Replay","description":"# [FCP2] Sessions: MeshSessionHello/Ack, Suite Negotiation, Key Schedule, Anti-Replay

## Goal
Implement the **session layer** that authenticates FCPS datagrams and secures FCPC frames.

The key idea: Ed25519 per-frame signatures are too expensive; FCP2 uses a one-time authenticated handshake to derive session keys, then MACs/encrypts frames with a monotonic sequence and a bounded replay window.

## Background & Rationale

### Why Session MACs Instead of Per-Frame Signatures

- Ed25519 signing is expensive at scale (especially on mobile devices)
- AEAD already provides per-symbol cryptographic integrity
- Session establishment amortizes signature cost over many frames
- Preserves "cryptographic attribution independent of transport" goal

**SECURITY NOTE (NORMATIVE):** Poly1305 is a one-time authenticator; using one Poly1305 key across
multiple frames is cryptographically insecure. FCP V2 therefore uses HMAC-SHA256 or BLAKE3-keyed
for session MACs and reserves Poly1305 for AEAD contexts only (where nonce uniqueness is enforced).

### Why Users Care

- High throughput with strong attribution and replay protection
- Debuggable: we can explain why a frame/handshake was rejected (bad MAC, stale seq, cookie missing, wrong suite, etc.)
- DoS-resistant: the stateless cookie path prevents responder resource exhaustion

---

## Normative Rust Structs

### SessionCryptoSuite

```rust
/// Session crypto suite negotiation (NORMATIVE)
#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Debug)]
pub enum SessionCryptoSuite {
    /// X25519 + HKDF-SHA256 + HMAC-SHA256 (tag truncated to 16 bytes)
    Suite1,
    /// X25519 + HKDF-SHA256 + BLAKE3-keyed (tag truncated to 16 bytes)
    Suite2,
}

impl SessionCryptoSuite {
    /// Compute session MAC for a datagram (NORMATIVE)
    pub fn compute_mac(
        &self,
        k_mac: &[u8; 32],
        session_id: &[u8; 16],
        direction: u8,
        seq: u64,
        frame_bytes: &[u8],
    ) -> [u8; 16] {
        let mut data = Vec::new();
        data.extend_from_slice(session_id);
        data.push(direction);
        data.extend_from_slice(&seq.to_le_bytes());
        data.extend_from_slice(frame_bytes);

        let full_mac = match self {
            SessionCryptoSuite::Suite1 => {
                use hmac::{Hmac, Mac};
                use sha2::Sha256;
                let mut mac = Hmac::<Sha256>::new_from_slice(k_mac).unwrap();
                mac.update(&data);
                mac.finalize().into_bytes().into()
            }
            SessionCryptoSuite::Suite2 => {
                *blake3::keyed_hash(k_mac, &data).as_bytes()
            }
        };

        let mut truncated = [0u8; 16];
        truncated.copy_from_slice(&full_mac[..16]);
        truncated
    }
}
```

### TransportLimits

```rust
/// Negotiated transport limits (NORMATIVE when used)
///
/// Used to keep FCPS frames MTU-safe and avoid IP fragmentation.
#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Debug)]
pub struct TransportLimits {
    /// Maximum UDP payload bytes the sender will transmit for FCPS frames to this peer.
    /// Default if absent: 1200.
    pub max_datagram_bytes: u16,
}

impl Default for TransportLimits {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self { max_datagram_bytes: 1200 }
    }
}
```

### MeshSessionHello

```rust
/// Session handshake: initiator to responder (NORMATIVE)
#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
pub struct MeshSessionHello {
    pub from: TailscaleNodeId,
    pub to: TailscaleNodeId,
    pub eph_pubkey: X25519PublicKey,
    /// Random nonce for replay protection (NORMATIVE)
    /// Binds this handshake to a specific session attempt.
    pub nonce: [u8; 16],
    /// Optional stateless cookie (NORMATIVE when responder requires it)
    /// Prevents responder resource-exhaustion by deferring expensive work
    /// (signature verification, ECDH) until cookie is validated.
    pub cookie: Option<[u8; 32]>,
    pub timestamp: u64,
    /// Supported crypto suites (ordered by preference)
    pub suites: Vec<SessionCryptoSuite>,
    /// Optional transport limits (NORMATIVE when present)
    pub transport_limits: Option<TransportLimits>,
    /// Node signature over transcript (NORMATIVE)
    pub signature: Signature,
}

impl MeshSessionHello {
    /// Build transcript for signing/verification (NORMATIVE)
    ///
    /// transcript = "FCP2-HELLO-V1" || from || to || eph_pubkey ||
    ///              nonce || cookie || timestamp || suites || transport_limits
    pub fn build_transcript(&self) -> Vec<u8> {
        let mut t = Vec::new();
        t.extend_from_slice(b"FCP2-HELLO-V1");
        t.extend_from_slice(self.from.as_bytes());
        t.extend_from_slice(self.to.as_bytes());
        t.extend_from_slice(self.eph_pubkey.as_bytes());
        t.extend_from_slice(&self.nonce);
        if let Some(cookie) = &self.cookie {
            t.push(1);
            t.extend_from_slice(cookie);
        } else {
            t.push(0);
        }
        t.extend_from_slice(&self.timestamp.to_le_bytes());
        t.push(self.suites.len() as u8);
        for suite in &self.suites {
            t.push(*suite as u8);
        }
        if let Some(limits) = &self.transport_limits {
            t.push(1);
            t.extend_from_slice(&limits.max_datagram_bytes.to_le_bytes());
        } else {
            t.push(0);
        }
        t
    }

    /// Sign the hello message
    pub fn sign(&mut self, signing_key: &SigningKey) {
        let transcript = self.build_transcript();
        self.signature = signing_key.sign(&transcript);
    }

    /// Verify signature against node's public key
    pub fn verify(&self, verifying_key: &VerifyingKey) -> bool {
        let transcript = self.build_transcript();
        verifying_key.verify(&transcript, &self.signature).is_ok()
    }
}
```

### MeshSessionAck

```rust
/// Session handshake: responder to initiator (NORMATIVE)
#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
pub struct MeshSessionAck {
    pub from: TailscaleNodeId,
    pub to: TailscaleNodeId,
    pub eph_pubkey: X25519PublicKey,
    /// Random nonce for replay protection (NORMATIVE)
    /// Combined with hello_nonce, prevents session confusion attacks.
    pub nonce: [u8; 16],
    pub session_id: [u8; 16],
    /// Selected crypto suite
    pub suite: SessionCryptoSuite,
    pub timestamp: u64,
    /// Node signature over full handshake transcript (NORMATIVE)
    pub signature: Signature,
}

impl MeshSessionAck {
    /// Build transcript for signing/verification (NORMATIVE)
    ///
    /// transcript = "FCP2-ACK-V1" || from || to || eph_pubkey || nonce ||
    ///              session_id || suite || timestamp || hello.eph_pubkey || hello.nonce
    pub fn build_transcript(&self, hello: &MeshSessionHello) -> Vec<u8> {
        let mut t = Vec::new();
        t.extend_from_slice(b"FCP2-ACK-V1");
        t.extend_from_slice(self.from.as_bytes());
        t.extend_from_slice(self.to.as_bytes());
        t.extend_from_slice(self.eph_pubkey.as_bytes());
        t.extend_from_slice(&self.nonce);
        t.extend_from_slice(&self.session_id);
        t.push(self.suite as u8);
        t.extend_from_slice(&self.timestamp.to_le_bytes());
        // Bind to hello for anti-splicing
        t.extend_from_slice(hello.eph_pubkey.as_bytes());
        t.extend_from_slice(&hello.nonce);
        t
    }

    /// Sign the ack message
    pub fn sign(&mut self, signing_key: &SigningKey, hello: &MeshSessionHello) {
        let transcript = self.build_transcript(hello);
        self.signature = signing_key.sign(&transcript);
    }

    /// Verify signature
    pub fn verify(&self, verifying_key: &VerifyingKey, hello: &MeshSessionHello) -> bool {
        let transcript = self.build_transcript(hello);
        verifying_key.verify(&transcript, &self.signature).is_ok()
    }
}
```

### MeshSessionHelloRetry

```rust
/// Stateless cookie challenge (NORMATIVE when used)
///
/// Responder can send this WITHOUT allocating session state or verifying
/// the hello signature. This prevents resource exhaustion from handshake
/// floods (similar to DTLS/QUIC HelloRetryRequest pattern).
#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
pub struct MeshSessionHelloRetry {
    pub from: TailscaleNodeId,
    pub to: TailscaleNodeId,
    /// Stateless cookie computed by responder (NORMATIVE)
    pub cookie: [u8; 32],
    pub timestamp: u64,
}

impl MeshSessionHelloRetry {
    /// Compute cookie for a hello (NORMATIVE)
    ///
    /// cookie = HMAC(cookie_key, from || to || hello.eph_pubkey || hello.nonce || hello.timestamp)[:32]
    ///
    /// The cookie_key SHOULD be rotated periodically (e.g., every 60 seconds)
    /// with a grace window for in-flight handshakes.
    pub fn compute_cookie(
        cookie_key: &[u8; 32],
        hello: &MeshSessionHello,
    ) -> [u8; 32] {
        use hmac::{Hmac, Mac};
        use sha2::Sha256;

        let mut mac = Hmac::<Sha256>::new_from_slice(cookie_key).unwrap();
        mac.update(hello.from.as_bytes());
        mac.update(hello.to.as_bytes());
        mac.update(hello.eph_pubkey.as_bytes());
        mac.update(&hello.nonce);
        mac.update(&hello.timestamp.to_le_bytes());

        let result = mac.finalize().into_bytes();
        let mut cookie = [0u8; 32];
        cookie.copy_from_slice(&result);
        cookie
    }

    /// Create a HelloRetry for a given hello
    pub fn for_hello(
        cookie_key: &[u8; 32],
        hello: &MeshSessionHello,
        responder_id: TailscaleNodeId,
    ) -> Self {
        Self {
            from: responder_id,
            to: hello.from,
            cookie: Self::compute_cookie(cookie_key, hello),
            timestamp: current_timestamp(),
        }
    }

    /// Verify a cookie in a hello matches what we would compute
    pub fn verify_cookie(
        cookie_key: &[u8; 32],
        hello: &MeshSessionHello,
    ) -> bool {
        match &hello.cookie {
            Some(provided) => {
                let expected = Self::compute_cookie(cookie_key, hello);
                constant_time_eq::constant_time_eq(provided, &expected)
            }
            None => false,
        }
    }
}
```

## Key Schedule (NORMATIVE)

Deterministic key derivation from ECDH shared secret:

```rust
/// Session keys derived from handshake (NORMATIVE)
pub struct SessionKeys {
    /// MAC key for initiator to responder
    pub k_mac_i2r: [u8; 32],
    /// MAC key for responder to initiator
    pub k_mac_r2i: [u8; 32],
    /// Reserved for future control-plane AEAD (FCPC encryption)
    pub k_ctx: [u8; 32],
}

impl SessionKeys {
    /// Derive session keys from ECDH (NORMATIVE)
    ///
    /// prk = HKDF-SHA256(
    ///     ikm = ECDH(eph_i, eph_r),
    ///     salt = session_id,
    ///     info = "FCP2-SESSION-V1" || initiator_node_id || responder_node_id ||
    ///            hello_nonce || ack_nonce
    /// )
    ///
    /// Including both nonces in the info string binds the derived keys to this
    /// specific handshake, preventing session splicing attacks.
    ///
    /// keys = HKDF-Expand(prk, info="FCP2-SESSION-KEYS-V1", L=96) split as:
    /// - k_mac_i2r (32 bytes): MAC key for initiator to responder
    /// - k_mac_r2i (32 bytes): MAC key for responder to initiator
    /// - k_ctx     (32 bytes): reserved for FCPC AEAD
    pub fn derive(
        ecdh_shared: &[u8; 32],
        session_id: &[u8; 16],
        initiator_id: &TailscaleNodeId,
        responder_id: &TailscaleNodeId,
        hello_nonce: &[u8; 16],
        ack_nonce: &[u8; 16],
    ) -> Self {
        use hkdf::Hkdf;
        use sha2::Sha256;

        // Build info string
        let mut info = Vec::new();
        info.extend_from_slice(b"FCP2-SESSION-V1");
        info.extend_from_slice(initiator_id.as_bytes());
        info.extend_from_slice(responder_id.as_bytes());
        info.extend_from_slice(hello_nonce);
        info.extend_from_slice(ack_nonce);

        // Extract PRK
        let hk = Hkdf::<Sha256>::new(Some(session_id), ecdh_shared);

        // Expand to 96 bytes
        let mut okm = [0u8; 96];
        hk.expand(b"FCP2-SESSION-KEYS-V1", &mut okm).unwrap();

        // Split into keys
        let mut k_mac_i2r = [0u8; 32];
        let mut k_mac_r2i = [0u8; 32];
        let mut k_ctx = [0u8; 32];
        k_mac_i2r.copy_from_slice(&okm[0..32]);
        k_mac_r2i.copy_from_slice(&okm[32..64]);
        k_ctx.copy_from_slice(&okm[64..96]);

        Self { k_mac_i2r, k_mac_r2i, k_ctx }
    }

    /// Get MAC key for a given direction
    pub fn mac_key(&self, is_initiator: bool) -> &[u8; 32] {
        if is_initiator { &self.k_mac_i2r } else { &self.k_mac_r2i }
    }
}
```

## Anti-Replay and Time Policies (NORMATIVE)

```rust
/// Replay protection policy (NORMATIVE defaults)
#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
pub struct SessionReplayPolicy {
    /// Allow limited reordering; MUST be bounded
    pub max_reorder_window: u64,       // default: 128
    /// Rekey periodically for operational hygiene and suite agility
    pub rekey_after_frames: u64,       // default: 1_000_000_000
    /// Rekey after elapsed time to avoid pathological long-lived sessions
    pub rekey_after_seconds: u64,      // default: 86400 (24 hours)
    /// Rekey after cumulative bytes to bound key exposure
    pub rekey_after_bytes: u64,        // default: 1_099_511_627_776 (1 TiB)
}

impl Default for SessionReplayPolicy {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            max_reorder_window: 128,
            rekey_after_frames: 1_000_000_000,
            rekey_after_seconds: 86400,
            rekey_after_bytes: 1_099_511_627_776,
        }
    }
}

/// Time skew handling policy (NORMATIVE)
///
/// Clock drift is inevitable (mobile devices, VMs paused, etc.).
/// This policy defines tolerances for timestamp validation.
#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
pub struct TimePolicy {
    /// Maximum tolerated clock skew when validating iat/exp
    /// and handshake timestamps (default: 120 seconds)
    pub max_skew_secs: u64,
}

impl Default for TimePolicy {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self { max_skew_secs: 120 }
    }
}

/// Replay window tracker for a session
pub struct ReplayWindow {
    /// Highest sequence seen
    highest_seq: u64,
    /// Bitmap of received sequences in window
    bitmap: u128,
    /// Window size
    window_size: u64,
}

impl ReplayWindow {
    pub fn new(window_size: u64) -> Self {
        Self {
            highest_seq: 0,
            bitmap: 0,
            window_size,
        }
    }

    /// Check if sequence is valid (not a replay) and update window
    pub fn check_and_update(&mut self, seq: u64) -> bool {
        if seq == 0 {
            return false; // seq must start at 1
        }

        if seq > self.highest_seq {
            // New highest: shift window
            let shift = (seq - self.highest_seq).min(128);
            self.bitmap = self.bitmap.checked_shl(shift as u32).unwrap_or(0);
            self.bitmap |= 1;
            self.highest_seq = seq;
            true
        } else {
            // Check if in window
            let diff = self.highest_seq - seq;
            if diff >= self.window_size {
                return false; // Too old
            }
            let bit = 1u128 << diff;
            if self.bitmap & bit != 0 {
                return false; // Replay
            }
            self.bitmap |= bit;
            true
        }
    }
}
```

## Session State Machine

```rust
/// Session state for a peer connection
pub struct MeshSession {
    pub session_id: [u8; 16],
    pub peer_id: TailscaleNodeId,
    pub suite: SessionCryptoSuite,
    pub keys: SessionKeys,
    pub transport_limits: TransportLimits,
    pub is_initiator: bool,

    // Anti-replay state
    pub send_seq: u64,
    pub recv_window: ReplayWindow,

    // Rekey tracking
    pub frames_sent: u64,
    pub bytes_sent: u64,
    pub established_at: u64,
    pub replay_policy: SessionReplayPolicy,
}

impl MeshSession {
    /// Check if session needs rekeying
    pub fn needs_rekey(&self, current_time: u64) -> bool {
        self.frames_sent >= self.replay_policy.rekey_after_frames
            || self.bytes_sent >= self.replay_policy.rekey_after_bytes
            || (current_time - self.established_at) >= self.replay_policy.rekey_after_seconds
    }

    /// Get next send sequence and increment
    pub fn next_send_seq(&mut self) -> u64 {
        self.send_seq += 1;
        self.send_seq
    }

    /// Check received sequence for replay
    pub fn check_recv_seq(&mut self, seq: u64) -> bool {
        self.recv_window.check_and_update(seq)
    }

    /// Get MAC key for sending
    pub fn send_mac_key(&self) -> &[u8; 32] {
        self.keys.mac_key(self.is_initiator)
    }

    /// Get MAC key for receiving
    pub fn recv_mac_key(&self) -> &[u8; 32] {
        self.keys.mac_key(!self.is_initiator)
    }

    /// Direction byte for MAC computation
    pub fn send_direction(&self) -> u8 {
        if self.is_initiator { 0x00 } else { 0x01 }
    }

    pub fn recv_direction(&self) -> u8 {
        if self.is_initiator { 0x01 } else { 0x00 }
    }
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

### Golden Vectors Required
- [ ] Hello transcript bytes
- [ ] Ack transcript bytes (with hello binding)
- [ ] Cookie computation
- [ ] Session key derivation (all three keys)
- [ ] MAC computation for both suites

### Testing
- [ ] Adversarial: missing/invalid cookie rejected when required
- [ ] Adversarial: replayed hello/ack rejected
- [ ] Adversarial: tampered suites/limits rejected
- [ ] Adversarial: mismatched hello/ack nonces rejected
- [ ] Adversarial: timestamp outside skew window rejected
- [ ] Fuzz target for handshake parsing and transcript verification
- [ ] Replay window tests with reordering scenarios

### Integration
- [ ] FCPS can consume session keys for datagram MAC
- [ ] FCPC can consume k_ctx for control-plane AEAD
- [ ] Rekey triggers work correctly in sustained sessions

### Structured Logging
- [ ] Log session establishment: session_id, peer_node_id, suite, decision, reason_code
- [ ] Log rejection reasons: bad_mac, stale_seq, cookie_missing, wrong_suite, etc.
- [ ] NO secrets in logs (never log keys, nonces used for crypto)

---

## Dependencies

- **Depends On**:
  - 1n78 Epic
  - 1n78.3 Canonical Serialization (Hello/Ack are canonical CBOR objects)
  - 1n78.4 Core Types (TailscaleNodeId)
  - 1n78.5 Crypto Primitives (Ed25519, X25519, HKDF)
  - 1n78.16 Tailscale Integration (NodeKeyAttestation verification)

- **Blocks**:
  - 1n78.12 Wire Protocol Data Plane (uses session keys for FCPS MACs)
  - 1n78.13 Wire Protocol Control Plane (uses k_ctx for FCPC AEAD)
  - 1n78.17 MeshNode and subtasks
  - 1n78.21 Conformance (golden vectors for session handshake)
